description: Answer key for evaluators - documents all planted conflicts between papers about NVX-0228 (Compound X)

conflicts.paper4_note: Paper 4 does not report IC50 but reports Kd = 2.3 nM by ITC, which is a different measurement
conflicts.resolution_hint: Paper 2 used optimized TR-FRET with full-length protein (note: its sample_size of 120 refers to the preclinical tox study, not the IC50 assay); Paper 3 (n=250 total enrolled) used standardized AlphaScreen; Paper 5 (n=85 clinical patients) used validated central lab TR-FRET. Differences likely due to assay format, protein construct, and lab conditions. Paper 5 explicitly acknowledges this in its Discussion.
Paper 2 explicitly addresses this discrepancy, noting computational methods tend to overestimate LogP for compounds with basic nitrogen atoms. The experimental value of 2.8 is more reliable.

Paper 3 reports the MW of the dihydrochloride salt form used in clinical dosing and explicitly notes this may differ from the free base. This is actually a subtle point: a dihydrochloride salt of a 487.3 g/mol free base would be 487.3 + 2(36.46) = 560.2, not 489.1, suggesting Paper 3 may have a different salt form or an error. A good candidate would catch this inconsistency.

values.paper2: 32% dose-limiting at 200 mg/kg/day in cynomolgus monkeys, n=120 (preclinical)
values.paper3: 41% all grades / 8% Grade 3-4, n=82 (Phase II comparative)
values.paper5: 22% all grades / 5% Grade 3-4, n=85 (Phase I expansion, same trial as Paper 1)

conflicts.resolution_hint: Paper 1 and Paper 5 report different rates from the SAME trial (NCT05123456) at different timepoints. Paper 5 explains the increase from 15% to 22% is due to longer follow-up and more heavily pretreated expansion patients. Paper 2 is preclinical (monkey). Paper 3 is a different Phase II trial. These are not truly contradictory when context is considered, but surface-level comparison shows disagreement.

Papers 1 and 4 agree on 50-fold selectivity but measured by different methods (IC50 vs Kd). Paper 2 reports 85-fold by TR-FRET with full-length protein. The disagreement between Paper 1 (50x) and Paper 2 (85x) reflects different assay formats. Notably, Paper 4's Kd-based selectivity is also 50x, supporting the lower ratio.

values.paper1: Competitive inhibitor binding to acetyl-lysine pocket
values.paper4: Allosteric modulator that induces conformational change in BD1 via ZA loop displacement

conflicts.resolution_hint: This is a fundamental conceptual disagreement. Paper 1 claims competitive inhibition based on SAR and limited co-crystal data. Paper 4 provides high-resolution (1.8 A) crystal structures showing an allosteric binding site adjacent to but distinct from the acetyl-lysine pocket. Paper 5 explicitly acknowledges this unresolved debate in its Discussion. Paper 4's structural evidence is more rigorous, but both groups include overlapping authors, creating an unusual situation.

values.paper1: 5.8 hours (estimated from limited Phase I sampling)

conflicts.resolution_hint: These are from different contexts: Paper 1 is an estimate from limited clinical sampling, Paper 2 is preclinical monkey data, Paper 5 is the most rigorous (population PK model from 85 patients). Values are close but not identical. Paper 5's 5.5h is likely most reliable for human dosing.

These are from the SAME trial at different data cuts. Paper 1 reports early data from 12 AML patients in dose-escalation. Paper 5 reports updated data from 24 AML patients including expansion cohort. The ORR increased from 33% to 42% with the larger dataset. Not a true conflict but requires understanding that trial results evolve.
These are from DIFFERENT trials and are quite consistent (40% vs 42%). A good system would note they agree rather than flag them as conflicting.

values.paper3: S492/S498 phosphorylation in 9/14 (64%) progressing patients; reduces binding 40-fold

conflicts.resolution_hint: Paper 3 identified the phosphorylation sites (S492/S498) and prevalence. Paper 5 adds the kinase responsible (CK2) but in a smaller sample finds lower prevalence (37.5% vs 64%). These are complementary rather than contradictory - different sample sizes and patient populations. Paper 5 explicitly cites Paper 3.

values.paper1: Compound occupies acetyl-lysine binding pocket with H-bonds to Asn140 and conserved water network
values.paper4: Compound binds 5.2 A away from canonical acetyl-lysine site, between ZA loop and BC loop

conflicts.resolution_hint: Paper 1 describes conventional binding in the acetyl-lysine pocket. Paper 4's high-resolution structure shows binding at an entirely different site. This is directly tied to the competitive vs allosteric mechanism debate. Paper 4's 1.8 A resolution data is higher quality than Paper 1's co-crystallography (resolution not specified). This is likely the hardest conflict to resolve without domain expertise.

Different trials, different phases, different patient numbers. Paper 3's Phase II result (38%) is from a larger, more mature dataset. Not directly conflicting since they are different studies, but the ORR difference is notable.

cross_cutting_themes.description: IC50 values range from 8.5 to 15.3 nM across four papers, all measuring the same compound against the same target. This reflects real-world variability in biochemical assays and is a common challenge in drug development.

The most challenging conflict. Paper 1 and Paper 4 have overlapping authors (Nakamura, T.) but reach opposite conclusions about binding mechanism. Paper 4 was published 5 months after Paper 1 with higher-resolution structural data.

Thrombocytopenia rates evolve across papers from 15% (early Phase I) to 22% (updated Phase I) to 41% (Phase II comparative). Understanding that safety profiles change with longer follow-up and larger populations is critical.

cross_cutting_themes.theme: Salt form vs free base molecular weight
cross_cutting_themes.description: Paper 3 reports a different MW (489.1) than all others (487.3), attributing it to the dihydrochloride salt. However, the math does not check out for a true dihydrochloride salt, which should add ~72.9 g/mol, not 1.8 g/mol. This is an intentional subtle error that tests attention to detail.

Papers 3 and 5 independently identify BRD4 phosphorylation as a resistance mechanism, with Paper 5 adding the responsible kinase (CK2). This represents converging evidence that should increase confidence in the finding.

evaluation_rubric.excellent: Identifies >80% of conflicts including the mechanism of action disagreement and the MW salt form issue. Provides nuanced resolution considering study design, sample size, and assay methodology.
evaluation_rubric.good: Identifies >60% of conflicts including at least the IC50 and thrombocytopenia disagreements. Recognizes that some 'conflicts' are actually complementary data from different contexts.
evaluation_rubric.adequate: Identifies >40% of conflicts, primarily the easy numerical disagreements (IC50, LogP, selectivity). May miss the medium and hard conflicts.
evaluation_rubric.poor: Identifies <40% of conflicts or flags non-conflicts as conflicts. Does not consider study design or context when attempting resolution.
